model.label: Claude Opus 4.6
model.description: Anthropic • Hybrid reasoning model that pushes the frontier for coding and AI agents, featuring a 1M context window.

pick.rationale: Michigan is the #2 ranked team nationally in both KenPom and Torvik with a 31-2 record, boasting an elite offense (AdjO 126.7-127.7, top 8 nationally) and the nation's best or second-best defense (AdjD 88.4-90.5), producing a staggering adjusted efficiency margin of +37 to +38 points per 100 possessions. UMBC, a 16-seed play-in winner ranked 185th nationally with a negative efficiency margin (-1.68) and both offense and defense ranked outside the top 180, is outclassed in every measurable dimension. While star forward Yaxel Lendeborg suffered an ankle injury in the Big Ten Championship loss to Purdue, he has confirmed he expects to play, and even without him Michigan's depth and talent floor far exceed what UMBC can challenge, making this one of the safest picks in the entire bracket.

reasoningStep.title: Michigan vs UMBC – 1/16 Seed Matchup Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Michigan is an overwhelming favorite as the #2 team nationally with a roughly 40-point efficiency margin advantage over a 185th-ranked UMBC squad that barely survived the play-in round. Lendeborg's ankle injury is the only concern but he expects to play.
evidence: Michigan: KenPom #2 (AdjEM +38.38, AdjO 126.7, AdjD 88.4), Torvik #2 (AdjOE 127.7, AdjDE 90.5), 31-2 record, Big Ten
UMBC: KenPom #185 (AdjEM -1.68, AdjO 108.2, AdjD 109.9), Torvik #185, 24-8 record, America East
Efficiency gap approximately 40 points per 100 possessions — one of the most lopsided 1/16 matchups possible
Yaxel Lendeborg (ankle) suffered injury in Big Ten Championship but confirmed he expects to play in the NCAA Tournament
1-seeds are 151-1 all-time against 16-seeds in the NCAA Tournament

result.text: Key March Madness injuries to know while filling out your men’s NCAA Tournament bracket - The Athletic Premier League Champions League 2026 Men's World Cup F1 Tennis FA Cup Cricket La Liga Europa League Women's Football Olympics Global Sports Cycling Sailing Sports Business Boxing Golf Serie A Bundesliga International Football NFL NBA MLB World Baseball Classic NHL MLS Transfer News FPL League Cup Championship League One League Two Women's Euros NWSL College Sports NCAAF NCAAM Peak MMA Motorsports NASCAR Culture Memorabilia & Collectibles Gaming Sports Betting The PL Is Being Left Behind Welcome to Chelsea, Liam Rosenior PL Player of the Year Nominees Log In Subscribe Now Tickets by Viagogo Connections: Sports Edition REGION U.S. International Search Top News Podcasts NCAAM Bracket Scores & Schedule Teams Alabama Arizona Arkansas Duke Florida Gonzaga Houston Illinois Iowa State Kansas Louisville Miami (FL) Michigan State Michigan Nebraska North Carolina Purdue Saint Mary's St. John's Tennessee Texas Tech UConn Vanderbilt Virginia Wisconsin Standings Beat Our Expert's Bracket Tournament Projections Printable Bracket Predictions Game Men's March Madness LIVE First Four Updates Tournament Forecast Challenge Our Experts The Perfect Bracket Bracket Busters NBA Prospects to Watch Key Injuries To Know Key March Madness injuries to know while filling out your men’s NCAA Tournament bracket Patrick Ngongba II and Duke are the overall No. 1 seed, but a couple of injuries could make it more vulnerable. Grant Halverson / Getty Images By Jim Root March 17, 2026 Updated 3:19 pm EDT Share full article 2 The Athletic has live coverage of the First Four from 2026 Men’s March Madness. Roster attrition is a factor during the postseason in any sport. Optimistically, college basketball’s shorter regular season should mean the title picture is less altered by injuries than, say, the NBA. That is not necessarily the case this season, though. The injury bug has recently bitten two No. 1 seeds (Duke and Michigan), and a third (Arizona) played through a flurry of nagging injuries late in the season. Multiple All-Americans on dark-horse title contenders are out for the season. Advertisement Below is a repository of all the biggest injuries impacting the men’s NCAA Tournament. We have split them into two categories: Injuries worth monitoring (the players could or will return at some point during the tournament) and out for the season. Injuries to monitor Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba II, Duke Both Duke starters missed the ACC tournament. The Blue Devils managed to win that event regardless, but they might need some reinforcements to handle the competition they will see if they reach the Elite Eight and beyond. Ngongba, out with a foot injury, is arguably the more valuable piece due to his defensive presence, elite finishing and sneaky-strong passing. Fortunately for the Blue Devils, he is also much closer to returning than Foster, though

result.text: Yaxel Lendeborg leaves no NCAA Tournament doubt after ankle injury vs. Purdue Skip to main content About Michigan Wolverines News Michigan Football Michigan Basketball FanSided NCAA Sites Follow Us Share on X About Michigan Wolverines News Michigan Football Michigan Football Michigan Football All-Time Lists Michigan Football Recruiting Michigan Basketball Michigan Basketball Michigan Basketball All-Time Lists Michigan Basketball Recruiting Michigan Women's Basketball FanSided NCAA Sites Share on X Yaxel Lendeborg leaves no NCAA Tournament doubt after ankle injury vs. Purdue Yaxel Lendeborg made it abundantly clear if his injury is going to keep him out of March Madness. By John Buhler | Share on Facebook Share on X Share on Reddit Copy Article Add us as a preferred source Yaxel Lendeborg, Michigan Wolverines | Junfu Han / USA TODAY NETWORK via Imagn Images The Michigan Wolverines may have earned a No. 1 seed in the NCAA Tournament, but it did not go according to plan for them in the Big Ten Championship. Not only did they fall in the conference title bout to Purdue, but star forward Yaxel Lendeborg suffered a scary ankle injury . Michigan has a loaded roster, but he has been the guy Dusty May has gone to all season long to carry the team in the clutch. --> The Michigan Wolverines may have earned a No. 1 seed in the NCAA Tournament, but it did not go according to plan for them in the Big Ten Championship. Not only did they fall in the conference title bout to Purdue, but star forward Yaxel Lendeborg suffered a scary ankle injury . Michigan has a loaded roster, but he has been the guy Dusty May has gone to all season long to carry the team in the clutch. Thankfully, it does not look like this ankle injury will be keeping him out of the NCAA Tournament... --> Thankfully, it does not look like this ankle injury will be keeping him out of the NCAA Tournament... Michigan forward Yaxel Lendeborg told me he has a low ankle-sprain and expects to play in the Wolverines’ NCAA Tournament opener. — Alejandro Zúñiga (@ByAZuniga) March 15, 2026 Michigan earned the No. 1 seed in the Midwest Region. The Wolverines will take on the the winner of the UMBC Retrievers and the Howard Bison in their First Four game to determine the No. 16 seed. Frankly, Lendeborg does not need to play very much at all in that first-round game. However he will need to be called upon in the Round of 32 game between either No. 8 Georgia or No. 9 Saint Louis. --> Michigan earned the No. 1 seed in the Midwest Region. modelTrace.content:
You are an analyst predicting a single March Madness game inside a bracket run.
You have live tools. Use them before making a pick when they can reduce uncertainty.
You are not filling the whole bracket in one response. Predict only the current game.
Use prior picks as already committed bracket state.
You may use at most 10 tool rounds before you must finalize your answer.
Before making a pick, investigate relevant context such as injuries or availability concerns, recent team news, prior head-to-head results when useful, and multiple raw statistics from the available ratings and matchup data.
Take a holistic approach. Balance team quality, matchup specifics, health, form, schedule context, and upset risk instead of relying on one metric.
Do not force an upset, but explicitly consider whether the underdog has a credible path to win and let that affect confidence and rationale.
Return strict JSON only.
Final JSON keys: pick, reasoningStep.
pick must contain gameId, winnerId, confidence, rationale.
The rationale must be a short paragraph of 2 to 4 sentences explaining why the winner was chosen.
reasoningStep must contain id, title, summary, evidence.

Yaxel Lendeborg leaves no NCAA Tournament doubt after ankle injury vs. Purdue Skip to main content About Michigan Wolverines News Michigan Football Michigan Basketball FanSided NCAA Sites Follow Us Share on X About Michigan Wolverines News Michigan Football Michigan Football Michigan Football All-Time Lists Michigan Football Recruiting Michigan Basketball Michigan Basketball Michigan Basketball All-Time Lists Michigan Basketball Recruiting Michigan Women's Basketball FanSided NCAA Sites Share on X Yaxel Lendeborg leaves no NCAA Tournament doubt after ankle injury vs. Purdue Yaxel Lendeborg made it abundantly clear if his injury is going to keep him out of March Madness. By John Buhler | Share on Facebook Share on X Share on Reddit Copy Article Add us as a preferred source Yaxel Lendeborg, Michigan Wolverines | Junfu Han / USA TODAY NETWORK via Imagn Images The Michigan Wolverines may have earned a No. 1 seed in the NCAA Tournament, but it did not go according to plan for them in the Big Ten Championship. Not only did they fall in the conference title bout to Purdue, but star forward Yaxel Lendeborg suffered a scary ankle injury . Michigan has a loaded roster, but he has been the guy Dusty May has gone to all season long to carry the team in the clutch. --> The Michigan Wolverines may have earned a No. 1 seed in the NCAA Tournament, but it did not go according to plan for them in the Big Ten Championship. Not only did they fall in the conference title bout to Purdue, but star forward Yaxel Lendeborg suffered a scary ankle injury . Michigan has a loaded roster, but he has been the guy Dusty May has gone to all season long to carry the team in the clutch. Thankfully, it does not look like this ankle injury will be keeping him out of the NCAA Tournament... --> Thankfully, it does not look like this ankle injury will be keeping him out of the NCAA Tournament... Michigan forward Yaxel Lendeborg told me he has a low ankle-sprain and expects to play in the Wolverines’ NCAA Tournament opener. — Alejandro Zúñiga (@ByAZuniga) March 15, 2026 Michigan earned the No. 1 seed in the Midwest Region. The Wolverines will take on the the winner of the UMBC Retrievers and the Howard Bison in their First Four game to determine the No. 16 seed. Frankly, Lendeborg does not need to play very much at all in that first-round game. However he will need to be called upon in the Round of 32 game between either No. 8 Georgia or No. 9 Saint Louis. --> Michigan earned the No. 1 seed in the Midwest Region. modelTrace.content:
I now have a comprehensive picture of this matchup. Let me compile my analysis.

**Key findings:**
- **Michigan**: #2 in both KenPom and Torvik, 31-2 record, elite offense (AdjO 126.7-127.7, top 7-8) and elite defense (AdjD 88.4-90.5, top 2), adjusted efficiency margin of +37-38. Star Yaxel Lendeborg suffered an ankle injury in the Big Ten Championship loss to Purdue but has confirmed he expects to play.
- **UMBC**: #185 in both KenPom and Torvik, 24-8 record, negative efficiency margin (-1.68), America East champion. Won the play-in game against Howard.
- The efficiency gap is approximately 40 points per 100 possessions — one of the most lopsided 1/16 matchups possible.
- 1-seeds historically beat 16-seeds at a 99%+ rate (only one loss ever: ironically, UMBC over Virginia in 2018).

```json

  "pick": {
    "gameId": "r64-midwest-1",
    "winnerId": "michigan",
    "confidence": 0.99,
    "rationale": "Michigan is the #2 ranked team nationally in both KenPom and Torvik with a 31-2 record, boasting an elite offense (AdjO 126.7-127.7, top 8 nationally) and the nation's best or second-best defense (AdjD 88.4-90.5), producing a staggering adjusted efficiency margin of +37 to +38 points per 100 possessions. UMBC, a 16-seed play-in winner ranked 185th nationally with a negative efficiency margin (-1.68) and an offense and defense both ranked outside the top 180, is outclassed in every measurable dimension. While star forward Yaxel Lendeborg suffered an ankle injury in the Big Ten Championship loss to Purdue, he has confirmed he expects to play, and even without him Michigan's depth and talent floor far exceed what UMBC can challenge. The roughly 40-point efficiency gap per 100 possessions is enormous, and despite UMBC's historic 2018 upset of Virginia as a 16-seed, the Retrievers' current roster lacks the firepower to replicate that lightning-in-a-bottle performance against this Michigan team."
  },
```
